name: Jódar, Peal de Becerro, and Quesada Loop
name_original: Jódar – vuelta por Peal de Becerro y Alto de la Colmena
description: This difficult 46.8-mile (75.3 km) road cycling route in Andalusia features 3349 feet (1021 metres) of climbing and passes castles.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Jódar traverse a landscape characterized by rugged, steep limestone terrain within the Sierra Mágina Natural Park. The region features significant elevation changes, with routes winding through vast olive groves at lower altitudes and transitioning into mountain forests of Holm and Gall oaks, and pine trees at higher elevations. This area offers diverse natural features, including notable mountains and varied topography, providing a range of road cycling experiences. Best road cycling routes around Jódar The most popular road cycling route is Jódar, Peal de Becerro, and Quesada Loop, a 46.8 miles (75.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 37 minutes to complete, connecting several towns through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Plaza del Pópulo, Baeza – Baeza Cathedral loop from Bedmar y Garcíez, a difficult 41.3 miles (66.4 km) path offering views of cultural landmarks and scenic landscapes. Local road cyclists also love the View of Castillo de Jódar – Castle of Jódar loop from Jódar, a 46.2 miles (74.3 km) trail leading through the foothills of Sierra Mágina, often completed in about 3 hours 47 minutes. Road cycling around Jódar is defined by rugged limestone terrain, vast olive groves, and mountain forests. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Jódar are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 7 reviews. More than 900 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Jódar's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Jódar?
A: There are over 20 road cycling routes around Jódar, offering diverse experiences from moderate to difficult. The komoot community has explored these routes over 900 times, rating them highly for their scenic beauty and challenging terrain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Jódar?
A: Jódar's road cycling routes are characterized by rugged, steep limestone terrain within the Sierra Mágina Natural Park. You'll encounter significant elevation changes, with routes winding through vast olive groves at lower altitudes and transitioning into mountain forests of Holm and Gall oaks, and pine trees at higher elevations.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Jódar?
A: While many routes in Jódar are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy road cycling route available. These routes typically feature less demanding climbs and offer a gentler introduction to the region's beautiful landscapes.

Q: What are some notable natural features I might see while cycling in Jódar?
A: As you cycle, you'll pass through extensive olive groves and ascend into mountain forests of Holm and Gall oaks, pine trees, and salgareños. The region is part of the Sierra Mágina Natural Park, offering breathtaking views of precipitous cliffs and high summits. You might also encounter natural wonders like the Oleander grove of Cuadros River or the amazing Zurreón waterfalls.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Jódar?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Jódar are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996236423/jodar-peal-de-becerro-and-quesada-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jódar, Peal de Becerro, and Quesada Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 75 km route connecting several towns, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31858542" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Castillo de Jódar – Castle of Jódar loop from Jódar&lt;/a&gt; offers a 74 km ride through the foothills of Sierra Mágina.

Q: What historical landmarks can I explore along the road cycling routes?
A: You can incorporate historical sites into your rides. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31858542" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Castillo de Jódar – Castle of Jódar loop from Jódar&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the ancient &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4753950" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Jódar&lt;/a&gt;, a well-preserved Moorish fortress offering panoramic views. Other routes might lead you near the magnificent &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4129334" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albanchez de Mágina Castle&lt;/a&gt; or the historic town of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7428751" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bedmar&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Jódar?
A: The region offers good cycling conditions for much of the year. While higher peaks like Pico Mágina can be snow-capped in winter, the lower-lying routes through olive groves are generally accessible. Spring and autumn provide pleasant temperatures, while summer can be warm, making early morning or late afternoon rides ideal.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Jódar is known for routes with significant elevation gains, catering to experienced cyclists. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24525810" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Jódar – View of Castillo de Jódar loop from Jódar&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 88 km route with over 1300 meters of ascent, ideal for those seeking a demanding ride.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Jódar?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from more than 7 reviews. Cyclists often praise the stunning and varied landscapes, from the 'sea of olive trees' to the rugged mountain terrain, and the challenging climbs that offer rewarding views.

Q: Can I find routes that pass through towns or villages with amenities?
A: Many routes connect various towns and villages, providing opportunities for breaks and refreshments. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996236423/jodar-peal-de-becerro-and-quesada-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jódar, Peal de Becerro, and Quesada Loop&lt;/a&gt; passes through several settlements where you can find cafes or other services.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of the Sierra Mágina Natural Park's highest peaks?
A: Many routes provide dramatic backdrops of the Sierra Mágina mountains. While cycling, you'll have views of peaks like Carboneras, La Golondrina, and Jódar mountain. The highest peak in the province, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4444835" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Mágina Summit (2,167 m)&lt;/a&gt;, also forms a dramatic backdrop for many rides.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that feature natural monuments?
A: Yes, some routes will bring you close to significant natural monuments. The region is home to the 'Pinar de Cánavas,' known for its exceptionally large, centennial Aleppo pines. You might also find routes near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5198955" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Cuadros River&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5198945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva del Agua de Bedmar&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: Stone tower remains of a medieval castle, The Mocha tower, is seven meters high. It is believed that its construction took place between the 13th and 14th centuries. Located in the center of Peal de Becerro. Good place for a quick coffee or a cold drink.

The small town is located in the Sierra Mágina Natural Park, surrounded by olive groves. The most dominant building in the village is the Jodar Castle, one of the oldest in the province dating back to 860 AD.

faqs.question: Is the Jodar – tour via Peal de Becerro and Alto de la Colmena suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this is a challenging road cycling route classified as "difficult" by Komoot. It covers over 75 kilometers with more than 1,000 meters of elevation gain, requiring a very good physical condition and cycling experience. Riders should be prepared for a demanding ride.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily designed for road cycling, but be prepared for varied surfaces. While much of it is tarmac, there might be unpaved or less-maintained sections, characteristic of some Spanish routes. You might even need to push your bike at times, especially in the mountainous areas of Andalusia.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This tour passes by historical landmarks. You'll have the opportunity to see the Torre Mocha of Peal de Becerro and the impressive Castle of Jódar. The general area also offers picturesque mountainous backdrops and diverse natural scenery.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The region of Andalusia generally offers good cycling weather for much of the year. Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) are often ideal, providing pleasant temperatures and avoiding the intense heat of summer. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route is named after Jódar, Peal de Becerro, and Alto de la Colmena, suggesting it connects these locations. While a specific trailhead parking isn't detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns of Jódar or Peal de Becerro to begin your tour. Look for public parking areas within these towns.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It primarily uses public roads and passes through areas that do not require special access permissions.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for road cycling and covers a significant distance on public roads. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, it's not typically considered a 'dog-friendly' activity in the same way a hiking trail might be. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure they are accustomed to long rides and road environments, and always prioritize their safety and comfort.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other paths. Notably, it covers about 22% of the "Andalucía - North Route (Província de Jaén)" and smaller portions of "[CIMA JA05] Puerto de Tíscar * Quesada" and "Camino antiguo de Quesada".

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging road cycling trip?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, you should bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling attire, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. Due to potential varied road conditions, consider tires that can handle less-than-perfect surfaces.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or rest stops along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through towns like Peal de Becerro and Jódar, where you can find cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel and rest. Plan your stops in these towns, as services may be sparse between them, especially in more remote mountainous sections.

faqs.question: Is the route a loop or out-and-back?
faqs.answer: Based on the tour data, this route is designed as a loop, starting and ending in the same general area, allowing you to experience the full circuit via Peal de Becerro and Alto de la Colmena.

waypoints.name: Torre Mocha of Peal de Becerro
